tasks.content: Place your fingers on frets 5-6-7-8 on the low E string. Keep your thumb behind the neck (halfway down, behind your middle finger). Curl your fingers so the tips — not pads — touch the strings. Play: 5-6-7-8, one finger per fret, across all 6 strings. Slow and deliberate.
• 📺 Learn the Spider Exercise (your daily warm-up for all 84 days): [richterguitar.com — Spider Exercise Guide](https://richterguitar.com/spider-exercise-guitar-the-crawl/)
Focus purely on hand posture today. Keep your wrist neutral — not bent inward. Play one note at a time, making sure each rings clearly with no buzzing. This is your foundation.

Just listen. Put on Hotel California and focus only on the guitar solo section (starts at 4:18). Listen 3–4 times. Try to hear where Don Felder's part ends and Joe Walsh's part begins. Don't touch your guitar for this part.

• 📺 Watch (understand the context of what you're working toward): [Taplature — Hotel California Solo Slow & Close Up](https://www.taplature.com/single-post/hotel-california-guitar-solo-lesson-slow-and-close-up)
Alternate between playing the chromatic crawl on the high E string (5-6-7-8) and the low E string. 1 minute each, switch back and forth.

Close your eyes. Mentally hear the opening notes of the Hotel California solo. Picture your left hand moving to the frets — even though you don't know the notes yet, the habit of visualization starts now.
✦ You have started. That's the hardest step. Be proud of Day 1.|
Spider crawl on frets 5-6-7-8. Today lead with Finger 1 (index). Move it across all 6 strings while fingers 2, 3, 4 stay planted on the 3rd string.

Vertical independence drill — Hold fingers 2, 3, 4 on the B string (frets 6, 7, 8). Now move ONLY finger 1 to each string, one at a time, while the others don't move. This "decouples" your fingers. This is hard at first — that's normal.
• 📺 Watch (finger independence exercises): [GuitarWorld — Spider Exercise Variations](https://www.guitarworld.com/lessons/spider-exercise)

Continue listening to the solo. Today, hum along with Don Felder's opening melody. You don't need to know note names — just internalize the phrasing with your voice. This is ear training, and it's crucial.
Alternate: Spider crawl 1-2-3-4 → rest 30 sec → vertical drill → rest → Spider crawl.
Visualize your thumb relaxing behind the neck as you play. Each finger pressing with the minimum force needed.
✦ Sore fingertips are a badge of honour. They will harden within 2 weeks.|
Spider crawl. Today lead with Finger 2 (middle). This decouples your middle finger from your ring finger — they naturally want to move together.

Learn B Minor Pentatonic Scale — Position 1. Starting note is B on the 7th fret of the low E string. Pattern (string by string): E: 7-10 \| A: 7-9 \| D: 7-9 \| G: 7-9 \| B: 8-10 \| e: 7-10. Play it ascending, then descending. Use alternate picking (down-up-down-up).
• 📺 Watch (B minor pentatonic explained clearly): [guitargpsmethod.com — B Minor Pentatonic Scale All Positions](https://guitargpsmethod.com/guitar-scales-b-minor-pentatonic-scale/)
Play through the B minor pentatonic (Position 1) slowly, up and down. Set a metronome to 60 BPM. One note per beat. The goal today is to simply remember the shape — not speed.
Alternate: Spider crawl → B minor pentatonic up → Spider crawl → B minor pentatonic down. Rest 30 sec between each.
Visualize the dots of the B minor pentatonic scale on your fretboard. Can you see them in your mind with eyes closed?
✦ This scale is the mother tongue of the Hotel California solo. You're learning to speak it.|
Spider crawl, lead with Finger 3 (ring finger). This is the hardest finger — it's anatomically connected to your pinky. Push through slowly.

Alternate Picking Drill. Set metronome to 60 BPM. Play the B minor pentatonic Position 1 with strict down-up-down-up picking. Every single note is picked. No cheating. Your pick should barely leave the string between notes.
• 📺 Watch (alternate picking fundamentals): [appliedguitartheory.com — Hammer-On & Pull-Off Exercises](https://appliedguitartheory.com/lessons/hammer-on-and-pull-off-exercises-for-guitar/)
Play the B minor pentatonic up and down at 60 BPM with alternating pick. Then try 65 BPM. Don't move to a faster speed until all notes ring cleanly at the current speed.
Alternate between: scale at 60 BPM → spider crawl → scale at 65 BPM → rest.
Hear the metronome ticking in your head. Hear each pick stroke: down... up... down... up...
✦ Alternate picking is the engine of speed. Build it correctly now.|
Spider crawl 2-1-4-3 (non-linear sequence — this is the "Drunken Spider"). Forces your brain to plan ahead, not just react.

Smart Fingers Drill — Place fingers 1 and 3 on any two frets. Move them simultaneously in opposite directions: while 1 goes up one string, 3 goes down one string, then reverse. This breaks hardwired muscle patterns.

Play B minor pentatonic Position 1 with alternate picking at 65 BPM. Then add this: pause on the lowest note (7th fret, low E) for 2 beats. Listen to it ring. That note is B — the tonic of the entire solo. Ground yourself in it.
• 📺 Watch (deeper scale understanding): [pickupmusic.com — Minor Pentatonic Scale Mastery](https://www.pickupmusic.com/blog/how-to-play-minor-pentatonic-scales)
Rotate: Drunken spider → scale at 65 BPM → smart fingers → rest. Keep each block to 2 minutes.
Picture the Hotel California solo beginning. Feel relaxed hands, slow and fluid motion.
✦ Five days down. Your nervous system is already rewiring. You just can't feel it yet.|
Spider crawl. Any variation you like — you've earned some freedom today.

Chord mapping. The solo repeats over these 8 chords: Bm → F#7 → A → E → G → D → Em → F#7. Find the ROOT NOTES of each chord on the 5th and 6th strings. Bm = 7th fret, low E. F#7 = 9th fret, low E. A = 5th fret, low E. E = open low E. G = 3rd fret, low E. D = 5th fret, A string. Em = 7th fret, low E. Play each root note slowly, naming the chord as you go.
• 📺 Watch (theory behind the Hotel California chord progression): [guitarmusictheory.com — Hotel California Solo Theory + Free Tabs](https://www.guitarmusictheory.com/hotel/)
Put on Hotel California. As the solo plays, tap your foot on beat 1 of each bar. Try to HEAR when the chord changes. You're training your ear to feel the progression — this is what real musicians do.
Play root notes of the 8 chords in time with a metronome at 75 BPM (the song's tempo). One chord per bar.
Mentally name each chord as you hear it in the song. Bm... F#7... A... E... G... D... Em... F#7...
✦ Understanding the chords means you'll never be "lost" in the solo.|
Spider crawl — your choice of variation.

Film yourself playing the B minor pentatonic scale. Watch the video back. Look for: Are your fingers lifting too high off the fretboard? (They should stay close — "quiet hands.") Is your thumb positioned correctly? Are you picking with small, tight movements?
Play through B minor pentatonic Position 1 at your best clean speed (probably 65-70 BPM). Then try improvising just 4 notes over and over — you're starting to "say" something with your guitar.
Rotate through the week's exercises: Spider crawl → scale → root note mapping → back to scale.
Review the week in your mind. What felt hard? What clicked? Self-reflection is practice too.
✦ One week done. The neural pathways are beginning to form. Week 2 builds on everything.|
Spider crawl 1-2-3-4. Now do it at fret 5 (easier stretches). Keep the rest of your warm-up here.

Hammer-on basics. Fret the 5th fret, G string, with your index finger. Pick it. Now "hammer" your ring finger onto the 7th fret — strike it hard enough that the note sounds WITHOUT picking again. The second note must be AS LOUD as the first. Practice: 5h7 on all 6 strings.
• 📺 Watch: [JustinGuitar — Beginner Hammer-Ons (free lesson)](https://www.justinguitar.com/guitar-lessons/beginner-hammer-ons-bg-1008)
• 📺 Also watch: [pickupmusic.com — Hammer-Ons and Pull-Offs Mastery Guide](https://www.pickupmusic.com/blog/guitar-techniques-hammer-ons-and-pull-offs)
Apply hammer-ons to the B minor pentatonic. Instead of picking every note, pick the lower note and hammer the upper note on each string. Play it slow — 50 BPM.
Alternate: picked scale → legato scale → spider crawl → rest.
Hear the difference between a picked note and a hammer-on. The hammer-on has a softer, more vocal quality. That's the sound of Don Felder.
✦ Hammer-ons are your guitar's "singing voice."|
Spider crawl, both 1-2-3-4 and the Drunken Spider variations.

Multi-string hammer-on drills. Finger 1 on low E, fret 5. Hammer-on finger 3 to fret 7. Move to A string: finger 1 on fret 5, hammer-on finger 3 to fret 7. Continue across all strings. Then switch to fingers 1 and 2 for half-step hammers (5h6).
• 📺 Watch: [Berklee Online — Hammer-ons, Pull-offs & Slides Guide](https://online.berklee.edu/takenote/guitar-techniques-hammer-ons-pull-offs-and-slides/)
Play the B minor pentatonic with hammer-ons on every string at 55 BPM. Make every hammered note ring as loud as a picked note. Record yourself and check.
Picked pentatonic → hammer-on pentatonic → multi-string drill → rest.
Picture your hammer-on finger dropping like a guillotine — fast and precise.
✦ Every great lick in this solo contains at least one hammer-on.|
Spider crawl.

Slide basics. Fret the 7th fret of the D string. Pick it. While maintaining pressure, slide your finger up to the 9th fret without picking again. The note should sing, not die. Key: maintain firm pressure the entire slide. Practice: slides of 2 frets (7→9) on all strings. Also practice descending slides (9→7).
• 📺 Watch: [hubguitar.com — Hammer-on and Pull-Off Exercises + Slides](https://hubguitar.com/technique/hammer-on-and-pull-off-exercises)
Listen to the Hotel California solo and try to identify every place where you hear a "glide" between notes — those are slides. There are several in Don Felder's opening phrases.
Hammer-ons → slides → spider crawl → pentatonic scale.
Hear a slide in your mind — that smooth, vocal "sigh" from one note to another.
✦ Slides are how guitarists mimic a singer's portamento. Romantic and expressive.|
Spider crawl.

Combine hammer-on + slide. On the G string: pick the 5th fret → hammer-on to the 7th fret → slide from 7th to 9th. This phrase (5h7s9) appears constantly in rock solos. Practice it on every string until it's fluid.
• 📺 Watch: [appliedguitartheory.com — Legato Technique Exercises](https://appliedguitartheory.com/lessons/hammer-on-and-pull-off-exercises-for-guitar/)
Apply the combined technique to the B minor pentatonic. Play: pick (low note) → hammer → slide → next string. Slow and smooth at 50 BPM.
Rotate through all three: slides alone → hammer-ons alone → combined → rest.
In the Hotel California solo, the very first phrase is a bend + slide. Hear it in your head.
✦ You're building the exact vocabulary needed for Don Felder's opening licks.|
Spider crawl. Today aim for 70 BPM.

Grace note hammer-ons. A grace note is an extremely fast hammer-on that happens almost simultaneously with the pick stroke — it sounds like a "flick" or "slap." Technique: pick the 6th fret → immediately (within a 16th note) hammer the 7th fret. The 6th fret note should barely be heard. These appear throughout the Felder section to add emphasis to "landing" notes.
Listen to the Hotel California solo intro and try to identify grace notes — they're the quick ornamental "plucks" before a main note. Listen for them at 0:10-0:15 of the solo section.
Grace note drills → regular hammer-ons → slides → spider crawl.
The grace note is a "decorative entrance" to a note — like a singer doing a quick run before hitting the main note.
✦ Grace notes are what separate a note-player from a musician.|

title: Theory Focus — The A# (A Sharp) Note  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl.

Find the A# note. The B minor pentatonic scale contains the note "A natural" (the minor 7th). But when the chord changes to F#7, you need to play A# instead. Find A# on the B string: it's at the 10th fret. Also on the G string: 11th fret. Also on the high E: 6th fret. Sit with these notes — play them slowly and hear how "tense" they sound. They want to resolve upward to B.
• 📺 Watch: [guitarmusictheory.com — Hotel California Harmonic Theory](https://www.guitarmusictheory.com/hotel/)
Listen to the solo. When the F#7 chord comes, try to hear that sharper, tenser note in Felder's melody. That's the A#. You're training your ear to hear harmonic color changes.
Play A# on the B string → hold it for 4 beats → then play B (7th fret, low E or open B string). Hear the "resolution." Repeat. Hear how tension resolves to home.
The A# is a "leading tone" — it pulls the ear toward home like gravity. Feel that pull in your body.
✦ Understanding this ONE note will make your playing sound intentional, not accidental.|
Spider crawl — push to 72-75 BPM today.

Speed burst drill. Play 3 notes as one smooth unit: pick the 5th fret → hammer 7th → slide to 9th (5h7s9). Do it at 60 BPM. Then do a 2-second burst at 80 BPM. Return to 60. The burst forces your motor cortex to find a more efficient path.
Record yourself playing the B minor pentatonic with legato (hammer-ons + slides). Compare to your Day 7 recording. You've already improved.
Rotate: speed burst → scale at 65 BPM → hammer-ons → slides. 2 minutes each.
Reflect on this week. The techniques you just learned — hammer-ons, slides, grace notes — are the exact tools Don Felder uses in the opening phrases. You're almost ready to learn them.
✦ Two weeks done. Your fingers are stronger, your ear is more alive. Week 3 amplifies this.|
Spider crawl at comfortable speed. Prioritize clean tone over speed.

Pull-offs. Fret both the 7th and 5th fret of the G string with fingers 3 and 1. Pick the 7th fret. Now "snap" finger 3 off the string — pull it slightly downward (toward the floor) so it plucks the string as it leaves. The 5th fret (held by finger 1) should sound clearly. Practice across all strings.

• 📺 Watch: [soundguitarlessons.com — Guitar Hammer-On & Pull-Off Tutorial](https://www.soundguitarlessons.com/blog/Are-you-doing-guitar-pull-offs-this-way-Guitar-Hammer-on-&-Pull-Off-Guitar-Technique-Tutorial)
Practice pull-offs on all 6 strings: 7p5. Go slowly. The pull-off note should be as loud as the hammered note. If it sounds weak, pull more assertively.
Hammer-ons → pull-offs → combined (hammer then pull: 5h7p5) → spider crawl.
The pull-off is a miniature guitar pluck — your finger IS the pick. See it that way.
✦ Pull-offs are the "down" to the hammer-on's "up." Together they give you flight.|
Spider crawl. Try the 3-1-4-2 sequence — unusual ordering builds independence fast.

Pull-off endurance. Play the B minor pentatonic descending using ONLY pull-offs. From high E (10th fret) pull to 7th fret → move to B string (10th fret) pull to 8th fret → G string (9th fret) pull to 7th → continue down. 10 times up and down. Your ring finger should be starting to feel the effort — good.
• 📺 Watch: [Berklee Online — Hammer-Ons, Pull-Offs & Slides for Guitar](https://online.berklee.edu/takenote/guitar-techniques-hammer-ons-pull-offs-and-slides/)
Play pentatonic ascending with hammer-ons, descending with pull-offs. This is how great guitarists phrase scales — not just up-down robotically. 60 BPM.
Ascending with hammers → descending with pull-offs → full scale picked → rest.
The Joe Walsh sections use rapid pull-offs in his fast triplet runs. You're building that endurance now.
✦ Joe Walsh's blazing runs are built on exactly these pull-offs.|
Spider crawl.

Vibrato fundamentals. Fret the 7th fret, G string, with your ring finger (fingers 1 and 2 behind it for support). Pick the note. Now oscillate the string up and down rhythmically — not randomly, but in a slow pulse. The pitch should rise and fall evenly. Key: the motion comes from your WRIST rotating (like turning a door knob) — NOT just your finger wiggling. On acoustic, you need wider movement than on electric. Be patient — this takes weeks to feel natural.
• 📺 Watch: [pickupmusic.com — Bends and Vibrato Technique Guide](https://www.pickupmusic.com/blog/guitar-techniques-bending-and-vibrato)
• 📺 Also watch: [JustinGuitar — String Bending Vibrato](https://www.justinguitar.com/guitar-lessons/string-bending-vibrato-te-107)

Hold any note in the B minor pentatonic and practice vibrato for 4-6 seconds. Move to the next note. The Hotel California solo LIVES in its vibrato — especially Felder's long, weeping notes at the beginning.
Vibrato on one note (10 sec) → pull-offs → vibrato on a different note → hammer-ons.
Hear BB King. Hear David Gilmour. Hear Don Felder. Wide, slow, aching vibrato. That's the goal.
✦ Vibrato is the difference between playing notes and saying something. Give it your full attention.|
Spider crawl.

Descending vocal slide. Fret the 14th fret of the high E string. Pick it and immediately slide DOWN to the 7th fret with firm, consistent pressure. The note should "sigh" all the way down — not stutter or die. This mimics a singer's voice falling at the end of a phrase. Practice on both B and high E strings: 14→7, 12→7, 10→7.

Listen to the Joe Walsh sections of the Hotel California solo. Hear those long, wailing descending phrases? Some involve exactly this kind of "vocal slide." You're recognizing techniques by ear now — that's a huge leap.
Ascending slide → descending vocal slide → vibrato on a held note → spider crawl.
A "vocal slide" is a guitar crying. Feel the emotion it carries.
✦ Joe Walsh's most powerful phrases are just these long, expressive slides with vibrato at the end.|
Spider crawl. Push for cleanliness at whatever tempo you've built up to.

Trill exercise. A trill is a rapid alternation of hammer-on and pull-off on the same two frets. On the G string: pick 7th fret → hammer 9th fret → pull back to 7th → hammer → pull → keep going for 30 seconds without stopping. Rest 30 sec. Repeat 3 times. This builds the endurance needed for Walsh's triplet runs.
Practice trills on different string pairs across the pentatonic scale. Focus on keeping both notes at equal volume.
Trill on G string → trill on B string → vibrato → pull-offs → rest.
See the trill as a washing machine — constant, rhythmic, controlled rotation.
✦ The trill is the foundational movement for every fast run in the Walsh section.|
Spider crawl.

8th-note triplets using pull-offs. The Hotel California solo's fastest section uses triplet rhythms. Pattern: pick the 7th fret of B string, pull to 5th → pull to open B (if available), or: 7p5-7p5-7p5. Set metronome to 60 BPM. One triplet = 3 notes per beat. Slow this down heavily at first. FEEL the 1-2-3 pulse within each beat.
Set metronome to 60 BPM. Practice the triplet pattern on the B string, then the G string, then alternate. Do not rush. Triplets sound wrong before they feel right.
Triplets → vibrato → trill → hammer-pull combination → rest.
1-trip-let, 2-trip-let, 3-trip-let, 4-trip-let. Feel the triplet pulse in your chest.
✦ Triplets unlock Joe Walsh's famous "deedily" sections. Week 10 perfects them. You're seeding them now.|
Chromatic spider crawl, all variations.

Record yourself doing all of this week's techniques: (1) Pull-offs across 3 strings. (2) Vibrato on a held note for 8 seconds. (3) Trill for 20 seconds. (4) Triplet pattern at 60 BPM. Watch back and note: Is your vibrato rhythmic or random? Are both pull-off notes equal volume? Are triplets even?
• 📺 Reference: [jamesshipwayguitar.com — Vibrato on String Bends](https://jamesshipwayguitar.com/bending-vibrato-guitar-lesson-how-to-vibrato-string-bends/)
Play through everything from Week 1, 2, and 3 in a flowing sequence. No stopping — just transition between exercises like one continuous piece of music.

You have now built: spider drills, pentatonic scale, alternate picking, hammer-ons, pull-offs, slides, vibrato, trills, triplets. These are exactly the techniques in the Hotel California solo. You're ready for Week 4.
✦ 21 days in. You have the entire technical vocabulary. Now we use it.|

title: The 1-3-5 Money Notes for Bm and F#7  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl + B minor pentatonic with vibrato on each long note.

"Money notes" are the 1st, 3rd, and 5th of each chord — they always sound great. For Bm: B (7th fret low E), D (5th fret A or 7th fret G), F# (9th fret A or 2nd fret high E). For F#7: F# (2nd/9th fret), A# (1st fret B or 6th fret high E), C# (4th fret A or 11th fret G). Find and play each of these notes. Hold each for 4 beats with vibrato.
• 📺 Watch: [guitarmusictheory.com — Hotel California Theory & Chord-Tone Maps](https://www.guitarmusictheory.com/hotel/)
Put on Hotel California. Every time you hear the Bm chord, play the B note (7th fret low E) and hold it. Every time F#7 comes, play the F# (9th fret low E). You're tracking the harmony in real time.
Bm money notes → F#7 money notes → vibrato on random pentatonic notes → spider crawl.
The solo "resolves" at the money notes. They're anchor points in a sea of notes. Picture them glowing on the fretboard.
✦ When you know the money notes, you're never lost in a solo.|

title: B Dorian Scale Over the E Major Chord  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl + pentatonic with legato.

When the E major chord appears in the progression, Don Felder uses the note G# instead of G. This creates a "Dorian" flavour — brighter and more hopeful than the regular minor scale. Find G# on your guitar: 4th fret B string, 9th fret A string, 1st fret high E. Practice the B Dorian scale: B-C#-D-E-F#-G#-A. The G# is the "sun breaking through clouds" moment of the solo.
Listen to the Hotel California solo. Every time the E major chord arrives, listen for that brighter, more "lifted" sound in Felder's melody. That's the G#. Try to identify it by ear.
Play B natural minor → switch to B Dorian (just raise the G to G#) → back to natural minor. Hear the difference. It's subtle but beautiful.
The E major chord is a moment of lightness in the otherwise melancholic progression. Hear it as sunrise.
✦ That G# note is one of the most beautiful moments in the entire solo.|

title: B Harmonic Minor — The A# Resolution  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl at whatever tempo is comfortable.

B Harmonic Minor is the B natural minor scale with the 7th degree raised: B-C#-D-E-F#-G-A#. Play this scale slowly (frets 7 through the neck). Focus on hearing how A# (the raised 7th) creates tension that resolves back to B. Play: A# (10th fret B string) → B (7th fret low E). That pull is harmonic minor's defining feeling. The Hotel California solo uses this every time F#7 appears.

Set metronome to 60 BPM. Practice switching between B natural minor and B harmonic minor (raising the 7th by one fret) every 4 beats. This is what Felder's brain does automatically during the solo — yours will too.
Natural minor → harmonic minor → Dorian → back to pentatonic (home base). A mini modal journey.
Think of the scales as emotional moods: natural minor = sad, harmonic minor = tense, Dorian = hopeful, pentatonic = soulful.
✦ You now have the entire harmonic map of the Hotel California solo in your hands.|
Spider crawl.

Learn the triad arpeggio shapes for the final section. Bm arpeggio (notes B-D-F#): on the high 3 strings (e-B-G), frets 7-7-7 (B), 10-8-7 (D-F# variations). Play one note at a time, letting each ring. F#7 arpeggio (notes F#-A#-C#): roughly frets 9-10-9 on e-B-G. These are the shapes that make the famous cascading finale.
• 📺 Watch (the full note-for-note lesson including arpeggios): [Desi Serna — Hotel California Solo Tabs + Lesson](https://www.guitarmusictheory.com/hotel/)

Listen to the final minute of the Hotel California coda (arpeggios section). Count each arpeggio. There are 8 chords repeating as arpeggios. Try to feel the cascading, ringing quality — that's what you're building toward.
Bm arpeggio → F#7 arpeggio → A arpeggio (A-C#-E: 5th fret area) → rest. Let notes ring into each other.
The arpeggio section is a cathedral of sound — each note is a stone placed precisely. Build it note by note.
✦ The arpeggios aren't just notes — they're architecture. You're already beginning to understand them.|
Spider crawl + pentatonic with full vibrato on every held note.

Ghost bend (pre-bend). This is THE technique of Don Felder's opening phrases. How it works: (1) Silently bend the string UP to the target pitch (don't pick yet). (2) THEN pick the string while it's already bent to pitch. (3) Slowly release the bend down to the original pitch. The result is a note that starts high and "cries" downward. Practice on the 7th fret B string: pre-bend to the pitch of the 9th fret → pick → release to 7th. The note cries downward like a voice saying "no..."

• 📺 Watch (ghost bends explained in Hotel California context): [guitarplayer.com — String Bending Workout (ghost bends section)](https://www.guitarplayer.com/lessons/string-bending-the-one-hour-workout)

Listen to the opening 10 seconds of the Hotel California solo. Every weeping, falling phrase you hear is a ghost bend. Felder uses them constantly. Practice the ghost bend motion until it feels natural.
Ghost bend on B string → regular bend on G string → vibrato on E string → rest.
The ghost bend is the guitar crying. It's one of the most emotional techniques in all of guitar playing.
✦ The ghost bend is the signature sound of the Hotel California solo's opening section. Master this.|
Spider crawl.

Practice switching between B natural minor and B harmonic minor at the chord changes. Set metronome to 60 BPM. Play 4 bars of natural minor → switch to harmonic minor for 2 bars (where F#7 would be) → back to natural minor. The switch is just one note changing: A natural becomes A#. Practice until it's automatic.

With the Hotel California backing track (or the actual song), improvise freely using the B minor pentatonic as your home base. When you hear F#7, try to work in the A# note. When you hear E major, try to work in G#. Even one "right" note per chord change is progress.
• 🎵 Practice with: [Songsterr — Hotel California Solo Tab (interactive, shows chord positions)](https://www.songsterr.com/a/wsa/eagles-hotel-california-solo-tab-s438376)
Scales → arpeggios → ghost bend → vibrato → rest.
You're becoming a musician who thinks about harmony while playing. That's the real transformation of Month 1.
✦ Improvisation with harmonic awareness: that's the goal of all guitar training.|
Full warm-up routine of your choice.

(1) Play B minor pentatonic with clean tone at 75 BPM — all hammer-ons going up, all pull-offs coming down. (2) Sustain vibrato on the 9th fret, B string, for 10 seconds. (3) Execute a ghost bend cleanly. (4) Name the scale/mode for each of the 8 chords without looking. Record all of this.
Improvise over the Hotel California chord progression for 2 full minutes using everything you've learned. Don't try to play the solo — just express yourself with the vocabulary you have.
You have completed Month 1. You have the physical hardware, the cognitive software, and the emotional vocabulary. Month 2 is where you learn the actual notes of the solo, phrase by phrase.
✦ One month done. You are ready. Month 2 is where the magic really begins.|
Spider crawl + ghost bend drills from Week 4.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): The solo opens over the Bm chord with a sustained bend on the B string. On the 10th fret of the B string, bend the note UP to the pitch of the 12th fret (a whole step bend). Hold it. Add slow vibrato. This is the first phrase. Use all 3 fingers supporting the bend (ring bends, middle and index behind it). Start at 30% tempo — excruciatingly slow. Get the bend in tune FIRST.

• 📺 Watch (the full slow-motion solo with close-up fretting hand): [Taplature — Hotel California Solo Slow & Close-Up](https://www.taplature.com/single-post/hotel-california-guitar-solo-lesson-slow-and-close-up)

Play the 12th fret of the B string (the target pitch). Let it ring and remember it. Now BEND the 10th fret up to that exact pitch. Match it. Your ear will tell you if you're sharp or flat. This intonation training is critical.
• 📺 Bending technique reference: [study-guitar.com — How to Improve String Bends](https://www.study-guitar.com/blog/how-to-improve-string-bends-and-vibrato/)
Ghost bends + regular bends + vibrato. Rotate every 3 minutes.
That first bent note with vibrato is a statement. "Here I am. I have something to say." Feel the intention behind it.
✦ The first note of the Hotel California solo is one of the most iconic in rock history. It's yours now.|
Spider crawl + Lick 1 from yesterday.

This is the most important session of Month 2. Use a tuner app (GuitarTuna is free). Play any note on the high E string. Bend it toward the next pitch. Watch the tuner — did you hit it exactly? Do this on the B string and G string. Your goal: consistently hitting the target pitch within 5-10 cents. Acoustic bending is hard — your wrist must rotate more than on electric.
• 📺 Watch: [guitarworld.com — Improve String Bends Exercise](https://www.guitarworld.com/lessons/bent-out-shape-improve-your-string-bends-simple-exercise)
Lick 1 again. Play it 10 times perfectly in a row. "Perfectly" means the bend hits the right pitch, the vibrato is rhythmic, and the note sustains clearly.
Lick 1 → ghost bend → pitch-matching drill → vibrato only (no bend) → rest.
Out of tune bends are the most common mistake in learning this solo. Commit to tuning your ear every single day.
✦ Bend intonation is the difference between "sounds like Hotel California" and "IS Hotel California."|
Spider crawl + Lick 1 with vibrato.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 2 appears over the F#7 chord. It introduces the A# note (10th fret, B string) and features a "stutter" rhythm — short, punched notes that create rhythmic tension. The phrase descends through the scale with some double-stops and bends. Learn it at 25% tempo from the Taplature lesson. Focus on the A# note — it defines the F#7 chord's character.
• 📺 Reference (with tab): [Taplature — Hotel California Solo Slow & Close-Up](https://www.taplature.com/single-post/hotel-california-guitar-solo-lesson-slow-and-close-up)
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Lick 1 → pause 2 beats → Lick 2. The connection between them matters as much as each lick individually. The "silence" between phrases is not empty — it breathes.
Lick 1 alone → Lick 2 alone → Lick 1 into Lick 2 → rest.
Felder is having a conversation with the chord changes. Lick 1 says "I'm here." Lick 2 says "but I'm restless."
✦ Every lick responds to its chord. You're not just playing notes — you're talking to the harmony.|

title: Lick 3 — Over A Major (Grace Note Slide)  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl + Licks 1 and 2 in sequence.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 3 falls over the A major chord and uses the B natural minor scale. It features a grace note slide into the first note — you approach the target fret from one fret below with a quick ascending slide. This "slide into" technique gives the phrase its smooth, elegant beginning. Learn it at 25-30% tempo.
Practice the slide entrance to any note. From 1 fret below, slide up quickly. This should take less than 1/16th note of time. It's a decoration, not a separate phrase.
Lick 1 → Lick 2 → Lick 3 in sequence. Slow tempo. Focus on breathing between each lick.
Lick 3 alone → Lick 1 → Lick 3 again → Lick 2.
Lick 3 is a gentle response — softer than Lick 2. The A chord brings a momentary sense of release.
✦ Notice how the phrasing changes with the chord? That's musical storytelling.|

title: Lick 4 — Over E Major (The Dorian G#)  \

tasks.content: Spider crawl + Licks 1-3 in sequence.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 4 falls over the E major chord. This is the "Dorian moment" where Felder plays the G# note — the raised 6th of B minor. The phrase has a lift, a hopefulness that contrasts beautifully with the melancholy of the Bm sections. Find the G# on the B string (8th fret) and high E string (4th fret). The lick ascends toward these notes then resolves back down. Learn it at 25-30% tempo.
G# FOCUS (10 min): Play ONLY the G# note. Hold it. Add vibrato. Listen to how it sounds against the E major chord — it's bright, almost optimistic. That's the emotional function of this lick.
Lick 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 as one connected phrase. You're now playing the first 4 bars of the solo.
The E major chord is a window opening in the middle of a dark room. Lick 4 is the sunlight coming through.
✦ You just learned 4 bars of one of the greatest guitar solos ever written. Let that land.|
Spider crawl + bend intonation check (use tuner).

Play Licks 1-4 in sequence, over and over. Set a timer — do not stop for 15 minutes. Every repetition, even imperfect, deepens the neural groove. After 15 minutes, rest 5 minutes, then 15 more. Count how many clean run-throughs you get in each 15-minute block. Write it down. You're tracking progress.
• 📺 Reference (slow the video to 0.5x speed): [Taplature — Hotel California Solo (use slow-down feature)](https://www.taplature.com/single-post/hotel-california-guitar-solo-lesson-slow-and-close-up)
Play the 4-bar sequence softly. Then again louder. The Hotel California solo builds from quiet and searching to passionate and intense. Begin training dynamics from the start.
You know 4 bars. 4 bars out of 16 (the main solo) plus the arpeggio coda. That's 25% done. You're a quarter of the way there.
✦ Consistency beats inspiration every single time. Show up. Play slowly. It accumulates.|
Spider crawl + Licks 1-4 as a warm-up themselves.

Play the 4-bar opening at 5 different volume levels: (1) barely audible → (2) soft → (3) moderate → (4) full → (5) as loud as you can while keeping notes clean. The actual recording has a specific arc — Felder starts moderately and builds. Practice that arc.
Try the 4 bars at 40% speed (up from 30%). If any bar falls apart, drop back to 30% for that bar only.
Lick 4 → Lick 1 → Lick 3 → Lick 2 (scrambled order — this forces your brain to "reload" each phrase independently, building stronger memory).
Felder is painting with a guitar. Light strokes, then pressing harder, building the picture. You're doing the same.
✦ Dynamics are emotion made audible. You're not just playing — you're feeling.|
Spider crawl + bars 1-4 of solo as warm-up.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 5 falls over the G major chord. It uses a rhythmic slide to connect the 7th and 9th frets on the D string. The slide must be rhythmically precise — it happens ON a specific beat, not as an ornament. Learn the exact placement from the tab.
• 📄 Follow along with: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Set metronome to 60 BPM. Play a slide from 7 to 9 fret EXACTLY on beat 3 of each measure. Precision of rhythm is as important as pitch.
Bars 1-4 → Lick 5. First time you've played 5 bars in a row. Take it slow.
The G major chord is where the progression starts to feel "epic." Lick 5 acknowledges this with a broad, sweeping gesture.
✦ Five bars. You're building a sentence, and the sentence is starting to say something beautiful.|
Bars 1-5 of solo as warm-up, plus spider crawl.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 6 falls over D major, the relative major of F# minor. This is a "resolution point" in the solo — the phrase feels like a temporary exhale. It focuses on D major triad notes (D-F#-A) and has a simpler, more song-like quality than the more complex Bm licks.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Find D (5th fret A string), F# (2nd fret high E or 9th fret A string), A (5th fret E string). Play them as a flowing melody, up and down. This is "thinking in triads" — very useful for soloing.
Bars 1-6 in sequence. One continuous phrase now.
Six bars. You can almost hear the full first cycle.
✦ The D chord is a rest stop on the harmonic journey. Felder takes a melodic breath here.|

title: Lick 7 — Over E Minor (Classic Pentatonic Lick)  \

tasks.content: Bars 1-6 + spider crawl.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 7 falls over Em and uses a classic B minor pentatonic phrase with a pull-off as its signature move. This lick is more aggressive than the Felder licks — it's transition energy, building toward the turnaround. Learn at 25-30% tempo with careful attention to the pull-off placement.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
The pull-off in Lick 7 must sound like a struck note — not muffled. Practice it 20 times in isolation: the pull-off note, just that note, over and over until it rings clearly.
Bars 1-7. Seven bars.
Em is the subdominant — it creates a feeling of "we're not home yet." Lick 7 reflects that restless energy.
✦ Lick 7 is the moment the solo starts to lean forward, into the turnaround.|
Bars 1-7 as warm-up.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Lick 8 is the turnaround — it falls over F#7 and uses the A# (harmonic minor) to create maximum tension that demands resolution back to Bm. This lick typically ends with a bend that "lands" you back at home. It's the most harmonically loaded lick in the first cycle. Learn at 20-25% tempo — this one earns its tempo slowly.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
The A# note creates tension. The bend that follows releases it. Practice just the last 3 notes of Lick 8 — the A# → bend → resolution — 20 times. Feel the gravitational pull back to Bm.
Play ALL 8 bars in sequence. One complete cycle of the Hotel California solo. This is a moment. Take it in.
You just played 8 bars of one of the greatest guitar solos ever recorded. Even at slow tempo, that is real. That is yours.
✦ You just played the complete first cycle. The entire solo is now one repetition of what you just did, plus arpeggios.|

title: The "Million Pound Challenge" — Lick 7 to Lick 8 Transition  \

tasks.content: 8-bar sequence once through, then spider crawl.

Isolate the transition between Lick 7 (Em) and Lick 8 (F#7). Play it 10 times perfectly. Not fast — perfectly. If you make an error, the count resets. This method (from Old Swanner's teaching philosophy) forces microscopic attention to the exact moment of connection. The transition between chords is where most players slip.
• 📺 Reference: [Taplature — Hotel California Solo Lesson (review the Em → F#7 section)](https://www.taplature.com/single-post/hotel-california-guitar-solo-lesson-slow-and-close-up)
Try the full 8-bar sequence at 40% tempo. How does it feel?
Lick 8 alone → full 8 bars → Lick 7 alone → full 8 bars → rest.
The turnaround is the moment of maximum harmonic tension in the whole solo. Feel it as your hands play it.
✦ Every great performance is built from solving small problems in isolation. This is the method.|
Spider crawl + bend intonation check.

Set metronome to 50 BPM. Play the full 8-bar sequence as cleanly as possible. No pauses. If you stumble, keep going — don't stop. When you get to the end, start again immediately. Do this for 30 minutes. You will play it many times. Each time is a repetition in your motor memory.
After the 30-minute block, try 55 BPM for 5 minutes. Does anything fall apart? Note what does — that's where tomorrow's practice focus should be.
The metronome is your accountability partner — it does not lie, it does not encourage you to skip. Trust it.
✦ At 50 BPM with clean execution, you are already "playing the Hotel California solo." The rest is just tempo.|
Full warm-up.

Record yourself playing the full 8-bar sequence at your current tempo. Then listen back critically: (1) Are the bends over F#7 in tune (A# is sharp, not flat)? (2) Is the vibrato rhythmic and wide? (3) Are the notes clean — no buzzing or muted strings? (4) Is there dynamic variety or is it all the same volume?
Identify the single weakest element from your recording. Spend 15 minutes fixing ONLY that element. This is deliberate practice in its purest form.
Recording and listening back is how professionals work. Every great guitarist has listened back to themselves thousands of times. You're doing the same.
✦ Recording is a mirror. Look into it honestly and your playing transforms faster than any other method.|

title: Walsh's Entrance Lick — Aggressive Whole-Step Bend  \

tasks.content: 8-bar Felder section (bars 1-8) as warm-up. You should be getting comfortable with these now.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Walsh enters over the Bm chord with an aggressive whole-step bend on the 9th fret of the G string, followed by hits on the B and E strings. The "hit" after the bend is percussive and staccato — shorter and sharper than Felder's style. Learn this lick at 25% tempo from the tab. Focus on the "stab" quality: Walsh speaks in exclamations. Felder speaks in sentences.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Play any note — then mute it with your palm after exactly 0.5 seconds. Pick → note rings → palm down. This builds the "punchy" staccato quality of Walsh's playing.
Bars 1-8 (Felder) → Bar 9 start (Walsh). Feel the personality shift in the music.
Felder = watercolour painter. Walsh = graffiti artist. Both on the same wall. Both gorgeous.
✦ Walsh's playing is raw power after Felder's elegance. Find that grit in your hands.|
Bars 1-9 of solo.

Double-stops — playing two notes simultaneously. Walsh uses these extensively. Basics: fret the 7th fret on BOTH the B and E strings simultaneously with one finger (index) lying flat across both strings (a mini-barre). Pick both strings together. Then add the 9th fret of the B string with your ring finger while keeping the index on the E string (7th fret). This two-note harmony has a thick, full sound.
Practice different double-stop combinations on the top 3 strings. Focus on both notes ringing clearly. On acoustic guitar, this is physically demanding — be patient.
Bars 1-10 in sequence.
Double-stops are two voices speaking simultaneously — a guitar harmony of its own.
✦ Double-stops give the solo its "both guitars at once" feeling even when playing solo acoustic.|

title: The Chuck Berry Riff — Rhythmic Double-Stop Lick  \

tasks.content: Bars 1-10 + spider crawl.

TODAY'S PHRASE (20 min): Bars 11-12 contain a rhythmic double-stop phrase over the A major chord that has a "Chuck Berry" quality — it's rhythmic, bluesy, and fun. Learn this lick at 30% tempo. It's shorter and more repetitive than the Felder licks, which makes it easier to memorize but requires rhythmic precision.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Set metronome to 60 BPM. Play any double-stop on the downbeat, mute, hit it again on the upbeat. Down-mute-UP-mute. This groove is the foundation of the riff.
Bars 1-12. Twelve bars of the Hotel California solo. That's the full first and part of the second cycle.
Walsh brings a rock 'n' roll swagger to the solo that Felder's classicism doesn't have. Both are necessary.
✦ You're now 12 bars in. The full solo is 16 bars plus the arpeggios. You're three-quarters there.|
Spider crawl + bend intonation check.

Pre-bend and release. Different from the ghost bend: (1) Silently bend the 9th fret of the G string all the way up (a full step to the pitch of the 11th fret). (2) Pick it. (3) Release it DOWN to the 7th fret pitch while still vibrating. The note starts high and falls — a "crying release." Walsh uses this extensively. The release must be controlled — don't just drop it. Pull it down slowly, like letting a balloon descend.
• 📺 Watch: [jamesshipwayguitar.com — Vibrato on String Bends Guide](https://jamesshipwayguitar.com/bending-vibrato-guitar-lesson-how-to-vibrato-string-bends/)
Practice pre-bend and release on G string and B string. Try different bend widths: half-step, whole-step, whole-step release to pitch below.
Pre-bend and release → ghost bend → regular bend with vibrato → rest.
The pre-bend release is controlled gravity. The note surrenders to the pull downward.
✦ Pre-bend and release: a note that falls like a tear.|
Full 12-bar sequence as warm-up.

Set metronome to 80 BPM. Play the B minor pentatonic (Position 1) ascending and descending with alternate picking. This is the highest tempo you've practiced at so far. If it's too fast and sloppy, go to 75. You must NEVER play sloppily at a high tempo — it trains the wrong patterns. Speed with accuracy. Accuracy over speed.

Play the pentatonic starting from different notes. Instead of always starting from the low E, start from the D string. Start from the G string. This prevents "scale tunnel vision" and makes the notes truly yours.
Try bars 1-12 at 45% tempo. How does it feel at a slightly higher speed?
Speed is a byproduct of relaxation. Tense hands cannot go fast. Breathe, soften your grip, and let your fingers find the notes.
✦ You cannot play this solo at full speed with tense hands. Relaxation IS technique.|
Spider crawl + bend drills.

The most difficult moment in the solo is the transition from Felder's melodic phrasing (bars 1-8) into Walsh's aggressive entry (bar 9). The rhythmic feel changes — Felder is legato and fluid; Walsh is punchy and percussive. Practice the transition specifically: play bars 7-8 (Felder's turnaround) and then immediately jump into bar 9 (Walsh's entrance). The transition is an energy shift, not just a note change.
FULL 12-BAR (10 min): Play bars 1-12 at 50 BPM. How does the personality shift between sections feel in your hands?
You are telling a two-part story — Felder's chapter and Walsh's chapter. The hand-off is the plot twist.
✦ The style shift between Felder and Walsh is what makes this solo legendary. Play both with conviction.|
Spider crawl.

Play bars 9-12 (Walsh section) with the actual Hotel California song playing. This trains you to feel the groove of the real recording — the pulse, the energy, the space. Stay at a slow internal tempo even while the song plays at full speed. You're not trying to match the speed yet — you're feeling the groove.
• 🎵 Use this for reference against the actual recording: [Songsterr — Hotel California Solo Tab (interactive)](https://www.songsterr.com/a/wsa/eagles-hotel-california-solo-tab-s438376)
Play your cleanest run-through of bars 1-12. Record it. Save it. This is your Week 7 benchmark recording.
Three weeks of the solo done. One week of the solo left. Then arpeggios. You're close.
✦ Twelve bars. Playing with the actual song. This is where it starts to feel real.|
Full 12-bar sequence, then spider crawl.

On an acoustic guitar, reaching the 14th-17th frets requires repositioning your whole arm — the guitar's body gets in the way. Practice: rest the guitar body differently (tilt the neck up more). Swing your elbow out. Let your thumb drop off the neck. This allows your fingers to reach the upper registers. Practice scales at the 12th-17th fret area just to feel comfortable there.
Play the B minor pentatonic starting at the 19th fret (high position). Work down from 19 to 7. Get comfortable. The upper frets are not scary — they just need deliberate body positioning.
Low fret pentatonic → high fret pentatonic → medium fret → rest.
The high frets are where the climax of the solo happens. Your body needs to be positioned for it.
✦ Upper fret work on acoustic requires smart body positioning — not just strong fingers.|

title: The 1.5-Step Bend — The Most Demanding Technique  \

tasks.content: High fret access drill from yesterday.

The 1.5-step bend (3 semitones) on acoustic is the hardest physical demand in this solo. On the 12th fret of the B string, you must bend the note up to the pitch of the 15th fret. CRITICAL: use all THREE fingers (ring bends, middle and index behind it on the same string). Power comes from WRIST ROTATION, not finger strength. Rotate your wrist like turning a doorknob. Your arm does the work. Start with a half-step bend, then a full step, then try the 1.5-step. Rest 2 minutes between attempts — this is physically demanding on acoustic.
• 📺 Watch: [guitarworld.com — String Bending Exercise for Accuracy](https://www.guitarworld.com/lessons/bent-out-shape-improve-your-string-bends-simple-exercise)
• 📺 Also watch: [study-guitar.com — How to Improve String Bends on Acoustic](https://www.study-guitar.com/blog/how-to-improve-string-bends-and-vibrato/)
If you feel pain in your wrist or fingers, STOP IMMEDIATELY. A little ache in the forearm from the supination motion is normal. Sharp pain is not.
The 1.5-step bend is a commitment. When you hit it in the solo, it's an emotional summit. Train for it accordingly.
✦ This is the hardest day of the 12 weeks. Be patient. Be safe. Even a half-step bend done beautifully is music.|
Spider crawl + 1.5-step bend practice (2-3 careful attempts).

Bars 13-16 contain descending scalar runs in 8th-note triplets — the "deedily" runs. Pattern: from the high E string, descend through pentatonic notes using pull-offs and alternate picking mixed. Set metronome to 60 BPM. Play one triplet per beat. Your goal today: just get the NOTE ORDER correct. Don't worry about speed. Notes first, rhythm second, speed third.
• 📄 Follow the tab closely: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab (bars 13-16)](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Set metronome to 50 BPM. Play one triplet (3 notes) per click. Count out loud: 1-2-3, 1-2-3. Feeling the triplet rhythm in your body is essential before it can live in your hands.
Triplet run (slow) → 1.5-step bend → triplet run → vibrato on held note → rest.
The descending triplets are a waterfall — gravity pulls them down the neck.
✦ Notes first, rhythm second, speed last. This sequence is non-negotiable.|
Full 12-bar sequence + bars 13-16 notes.

Some of Walsh's notes in bars 13-16 are "choked" — played short and muted for rhythmic effect. How: play the note, then immediately lay the palm of your picking hand lightly across the strings. Or press the fretting finger flat to stop the vibration. Practice: play a note at the 12th fret → let it ring 0.25 seconds → mute. The staccato quality gives the end of the solo its spiky, intense energy.
Play 4 notes of the pentatonic scale with staccato muting on each. Then play the same 4 notes legato (let ring). Switch back and forth. Hear the difference.
Bars 13-16 with staccato notes in their correct places. Very slow.
Staccato is punctuation. Short, sharp notes at the climax are exclamation points.
✦ Muting is a playing technique, not an accident. Walsh uses it as a musical tool.|
Spider crawl + upper fret work.

Set metronome to 40 BPM. Play ONLY bars 13-16, over and over. 40 BPM is aggressively slow. Every note should ring cleanly. Every bend should be in tune. Every staccato should be crisp. Repeat 15 times. This is myelination at work — perfect repetition wrapping the neural pathways in speed-boosting insulation.
After 30 minutes at 40 BPM, try 45 BPM for 5 minutes. Then 50 BPM for 5 minutes. Stop at whatever tempo introduces sloppiness.
Bars 1-4 → Bars 13-16 → Bars 5-8 → Bars 13-16. Jumping around forces each section to be accessed "cold."
Every repetition today is a brushstroke on the neural canvas. This session matters enormously.
✦ Forty beats per minute. That's not practicing slowly — that's practicing perfectly. These are different things.|
Spider crawl + gentle stretch of fretting hand.

Play all 16 bars of the main Hotel California solo (not the arpeggios yet) in sequence. Do this at the slowest tempo where you can keep going without stopping. Set a timer. Do it continuously for 30 minutes. Every run-through counts. Every imperfection is information about what to fix next.
Record one full run-through. This is your Month 2 benchmark. Save it.
You have played the main body of the Hotel California solo. End to end. At slow tempo. That is real. That is an achievement.
✦ Sixteen bars of Hotel California. All of it. In your hands. On acoustic. Take a moment.|
16-bar run-through as warm-up.

Play the actual Hotel California recording. Then play your recording. Do NOT be harsh — be analytical. Identify exactly 3 things that are different (pitch? rhythm? vibrato width? bend accuracy?). Write them down. These are your Week 9 focus areas.
Take the ONE biggest difference you identified and spend 15 minutes on just that element.
You are entering Month 3. You have the entire solo's notes in your hands. Month 3 is about: polishing, arpeggios, speed, and emotional performance.
✦ The gap between your version and the original is the roadmap for Month 3. It's not a problem — it's a plan.|
16-bar solo as warm-up at comfortable tempo.

The arpeggio section follows a repeating descending pattern: approximately frets 10-7-8 on the B and E strings. This "descending 3rd" pattern creates the cascading waterfall effect. Start with just the Bm arpeggio: B (7th fret, high E or B string), D (3rd fret B string or 7th fret G), F# (2nd fret high E). Play each note individually, let each ring before moving. Economy picking (continued picking direction when crossing strings) helps with speed.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab (arpeggio section)](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Play ONLY the Bm arpeggio shape, 20 times. Let all notes ring into each other like a harp. This "let ring" quality is the arpeggio's defining character.
An arpeggio is a chord spread out in time — instead of all at once, each note blooms sequentially. Hear it as musical patience.
✦ The arpeggio section is where the two guitarists become one voice. Pure magic.|
16-bar solo run-through + Bm arpeggio from Day 57.

TODAY'S SHAPES (25 min): Bm arpeggio (B-D-F#) on the top 3 strings. Then F#7 arpeggio (F#-A#-C#). Focus on the pull-off from the 10th to 7th fret of the high E string — this appears in the descending arpeggio figure. Practice each shape slowly. Then practice transitioning from Bm to F#7. These two arpeggios are the hardest shapes of the coda.
• 📄 Tab: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Play Bm arpeggio → hold each note as long as possible → the notes start to create a chord-like wash of sound. That's the effect you want.
Bm arpeggio → F#7 arpeggio → back to Bm → rest.
The A# note in the F#7 arpeggio is the same tension-builder you learned in Month 1. Now it lives inside a beautiful waterfall of notes.
✦ These two arpeggios are the heart of the coda. Get them right.|

title: A Major and E Major Arpeggios  \

tasks.content: Bm + F#7 arpeggios + 8-bar Felder section.

TODAY'S SHAPES (25 min): A major arpeggio (A-C#-E): roughly 5th-9th fret area on the top 3 strings. E major arpeggio (E-G#-B): 4th-7th fret area. Learn each shape. Practice the shift DOWN the neck from the 10th fret area (F#7) to the 9th fret area (A). These descend the neck, creating the "falling" feeling of the coda.
• 📄 Tab: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Bm arpeggio → F#7 → A major → E major. Four chords in sequence.
A major arpeggio alone → E major alone → combined → rest.
The arpeggios descend the neck like water flowing downhill — naturally, inevitably.
✦ Four arpeggios down, four to go. You're halfway through the coda.|

title: G Major and D Major Arpeggios  \

tasks.content: All four arpeggios from Days 58-59 as warm-up.

TODAY'S SHAPES (25 min): G major arpeggio (G-B-D): 7th-8th fret area on top strings. D major arpeggio (D-F#-A): varies by position. Also incorporate the G string notes — the G string is crucial for full-sounding arpeggios in this section. Practice each shape. Then Bm → F#7 → A → E → G → D in sequence.
• 📄 Tab: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Bm → F#7 → A → E → G → D slowly, letting each arpeggio ring.
G alone → D alone → combined → rest.
Six chords flowing. The progression is recognizable now even in arpeggio form. You can hear the song in it.
✦ Six of eight arpeggios. You're close to the complete coda.|

title: E Minor and Final F#7 Arpeggios  \

tasks.content: All six arpeggios in sequence + spider crawl.

TODAY'S SHAPES (20 min): E minor arpeggio (E-G-B): 7th-8th fret area. Final F#7 (same as bar 2 arpeggio — this closes the cycle back to Bm). Learn the "big stretch" from the 7th fret to the 3rd fret that appears in some acoustic arrangements here.
• 📄 Tab: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Play all 8 arpeggios in sequence. Bm → F#7 → A → E → G → D → Em → F#7. Slowly. One full cycle without stopping.
Em alone → F#7 alone → final 4 chords → rest.
All eight arpeggios. The complete coda structure is in your hands. This is the moment.
✦ All eight arpeggios. The complete Hotel California coda structure. Yours.|
16-bar solo run-through.

Play all 8 arpeggios without stopping. Then immediately restart and play again. And again. Do this for 30 minutes. The arpeggios become fluent when you can play the full cycle continuously without "re-learning" the next shape. You start to "see ahead" to the next chord while playing the current one.
Try the full arpeggio cycle at a slightly faster tempo (60 BPM). Does any shape collapse? That's the shape to isolate tomorrow.
Full cycle → single weakest chord repeated 5 times → full cycle → rest.
The arpeggio cycle repeating is a sonic spiral. Each cycle tightens the beauty.
✦ Continuity is the goal. Not individual shapes — the flowing, unbroken cycle.|

title: Rhythmic Accuracy — Arpeggio as Eighth Notes  \

tasks.content: Arpeggio cycle + spider crawl.

Set metronome to 50 BPM. Play each arpeggio note as a precise eighth note (2 notes per click). The cascading effect only works when the notes are rhythmically even — not lumpy. Count aloud: 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and. Each note falls on exactly one of those syllables.
Where does the rhythm become uneven? Usually at the transitions between chords. Isolate that transition and drill it until both chords flow evenly.
Record one full arpeggio cycle. Does it sound like the recording's cascading quality?
Even rhythm in arpeggios creates the illusion of two guitars. Your single acoustic sounds orchestral when the timing is perfect.
✦ Rhythm is the skeleton of music. Without even rhythm, the arpeggios are just random notes.|
Full arpeggio cycle at 50 BPM.

"Let ring" means your fingers stay arched and pressed down so each string continues vibrating after you move to the next string. On acoustic, this creates a harp-like wash of sound. Practice: play the Bm arpeggio shape — after picking each string, DO NOT lift any finger. All strings ring simultaneously. This requires very curved, arched fingers — flat fingers mute adjacent strings. Drill this for the Bm and F#7 shapes first, then extend to all 8.

Play arpeggios with notes NOT ringing (muted between notes). Then play with full let-ring. Hear the difference? The let-ring version is what makes the recording sound as if there are 4 guitars playing.
Arpeggio with let-ring → 16-bar solo (bars 1-8) → arpeggios again → rest.
Arpeggios with let-ring are a sonic cathedral — each note is a stone pillar holding up the sound.
✦ Let-ring transforms a sequence of notes into a chord in time. This is the magic of the coda.|
Let-ring arpeggios + spider crawl.

Economy picking — when moving from one string to an adjacent lower string, your pick continues in the SAME direction instead of alternating. Example: pick the high E string downward → immediately continue downward to the B string (instead of upstroking back). This eliminates wasted movement when playing cascading patterns. For the arpeggio section, economy picking is more efficient than strict alternate picking.
Practice the Bm arpeggio with economy picking on the string crosses. Feel the difference — it should feel more "flowing" and natural for descending runs.
Try the full arpeggio cycle with economy picking at 65 BPM. Did it speed up?
Economy picking is like water flowing downhill — the path of least resistance.
✦ Economy picking is the secret weapon for making arpeggios sound effortless.|

title: Integrating the Harmony — Primary Melody Focus  \

tasks.content: Full 16-bar solo run-through at current tempo.

On the original recording, both guitarists play arpeggios harmonized in thirds. For solo acoustic, you play the "higher" or "melody" note of the two-guitar harmony — this is the note that carries the recognizable tune. Identify which note in each arpeggio shape is the highest pitch — that's your melody note. Practice playing JUST those melody notes in sequence. Then add the supporting notes back in.
• 📺 Reference (hear the harmonized arpeggios clearly): [Licklibrary — Hotel California Solo Complete Lesson](https://www.licklibrary.com/learn/lessons/eagles/hotel-california-solo)
Sing the arpeggio melody while playing it. If you can sing it, you understand where the melody lives.
You're playing both parts of a duet simultaneously on one guitar. That's a beautiful thing.
✦ The melody of the arpeggios is the emotional centerpiece of the entire Hotel California coda.|
Arpeggio cycle with economy picking.

Between some arpeggios, there's a quick slide on the B string that connects one chord's shape to the next. This "staircase" slide makes the transition smooth and vocal. Practice: after playing the Bm arpeggio shape, slide the B string note down to the starting position of the F#7 arpeggio. The slide should be in time — it happens within the rhythmic space between arpeggios.
• 📄 Tab shows exact slide positions: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Practice the transitions between the 4 main arpeggio pairs: Bm→F#7, F#7→A, A→E, E→G. Each transition uses a slight repositioning slide.
The staircase slides are the footsteps between rooms — necessary, elegant, unnoticeable when done perfectly.
✦ Smooth transitions between arpeggios make the coda sound like one seamless flow.|

title: Dynamics in the Climax — Dig In!  \

tasks.content: Full arpeggio cycle with let-ring.

The arpeggio section is the LOUDEST part of the solo — it's the emotional peak. Dig your pick into the strings harder. On acoustic guitar, increased pick pressure = louder attack and more brightness. Practice the full arpeggio cycle at 3 volume levels: soft (exploring), medium (building), loud (arriving). The recorded version settles at "loud and present" for the full coda.

If you're playing with a thin pick (0.5mm or less), the arpeggios will sound thin and muted. Try a pick of 0.73mm or thicker. The pick thickness literally changes the sound of the arpeggios on acoustic.
Soft arpeggios → loud arpeggios → 16-bar solo → loud arpeggios.
The climax of Hotel California is pure release — all that harmonic tension finally letting go in a cascade of notes. Play it like you mean it.
✦ The arpeggios aren't quiet background — they're the emotional summit of the whole song.|
Spider crawl + gentle stretching of right hand (picking hand tends to tighten during arpeggios).

Play the full arpeggio cycle 5 consecutive times without stopping. In a real performance, the Eagles repeat the coda many times as it fades out. You need to be able to sustain this section for 2+ minutes. If your picking hand tires, adjust your grip slightly. If your fretting hand cramps, ease the pressure — you may be gripping too hard.
After the endurance set, try the arpeggio cycle at 70 BPM. Can you maintain let-ring at this speed?
Endurance is stamina. Musical stamina is built the same way physical stamina is — by going a little further each session.
✦ Playing beautifully once is skill. Playing beautifully for 2 minutes is mastery.|
16-bar solo run-through.

Record yourself playing the full arpeggio section (all 8 chords × 2 full cycles). Listen back and check: Are all notes clearly ringing (no buzzing or muting)? Are the transitions smooth? Is the rhythm even? Is the dynamic level consistent?
Address the single weakest element from your recording. Isolate and drill it.
Two more weeks. Week 11 is the outro and full-solo connection. Week 12 is performance tempo. The end is in sight.
✦ Ten weeks done. You have the complete Hotel California solo — main body + arpeggio coda. Weeks 11-12 polish it to a shine.|

title: The Twin Lead Outro — Descending Scalar Run  \

tasks.content: Full 16-bar solo + arpeggio cycle as warm-up. You're playing almost the complete solo as a warm-up now.

After the arpeggio cycle, the solo continues with a descending scalar run over the B natural minor scale. This run sequences down the neck, phrase by phrase. Learn this section from the tab at 25% tempo. It's less complicated than the main solo — more straightforward scale runs — but requires the confidence of knowing exactly where you are on the neck.
• 📄 Tab reference: [Ultimate-Guitar — Hotel California Solo Tab (outro section)](https://tabs.ultimate-guitar.com/tab/eagles/hotel-california-tabs-96835)
Arpeggio cycle → outro descending run. Practice the transition from the last arpeggio into the first outro phrase.
The outro is the denouement of the story — the telling winds down after the emotional summit of the arpeggios.
✦ Almost there. The outro is the final chapter of the greatest guitar story in rock.|

title: Final Resolution — Long-Held Note with Vibrato  \

tasks.content: 16-bar solo + arpeggios.

The solo ends on a long, held note at approximately the 14th fret of the high E string, with strong, wide vibrato that sustains for 4-6 seconds. This is your final statement. Practice it today: fret the 14th fret high E, pick it with conviction, and apply your best wide vibrato. Let it sustain as long as your acoustic allows. Then decay naturally. This note should feel like an exhale after a long journey.
Set a timer for 10 seconds. Hold the note. Can your vibrato stay rhythmic and wide for the full 10 seconds? If it falters at 4-5 seconds, your vibrato muscles still need development. Keep practicing.
Final note → arpeggio cycle → outro run → final note again.
The final note of Hotel California is an invitation — "the door is always open but you're never truly free." Let the vibrato carry that ambiguity.
✦ This single note, held with perfect vibrato, is more powerful than 100 fast notes.|
Full solo (all sections) as warm-up.

The Hotel California studio recording fades out during the coda. Practice playing the arpeggio section progressively softer — start at your full acoustic volume, then gradually reduce pick pressure over the course of 8 cycles until the notes are barely audible. This is excellent dynamics control and makes a live performance feel complete.

Practice the entire arpeggio section at the softest possible volume where notes still ring clearly. Minimum pressure technique. This also tests whether you're using too much grip — if notes die at soft playing, you need less grip, not more.
A fade-out is a musical sunset — slow, gentle, inevitable. Replicate it.
✦ Controlling your fading volume over 8 arpeggio cycles is a performance-level skill.|

title: The Hardest Transition — Arpeggios to Outro  \

tasks.content: Arpeggio cycle × 2 with let-ring.

The most difficult transition in the complete solo is from the arpeggio cascade into the outro scalar run. The energy shift, the position change, and the tempo feel all change simultaneously. Use the "Million Pound Challenge" approach: isolate the last arpeggio → transition → first phrase of outro. Play this 10 times perfectly. Do not move on until you achieve 10 clean repetitions.
Play the entire Hotel California solo from bar 1 through the outro, without stopping. How does it feel as one complete performance?
Every transition you've mastered this week connects one world to another. The arpeggios are the peak of a mountain; the outro is walking down the other side.
✦ Connection is the final craft. The notes are all there — now weave them seamlessly.|

title: Tone Audit — Pick Thickness & Acoustic Setup  \

tasks.content: Full solo run-through.

Today check your setup: (1) Are you using light enough strings? (Extra Light 10-47 or Custom Light 11-52 are recommended for acoustic bending.) (2) Is your action too high? (High action makes bends exponentially harder — a setup by a guitar tech is worth considering.) (3) What pick are you using? A medium-thick pick (0.73-1.0mm) gives the most control for both delicate arpeggios and powerful bends.

Try playing the arpeggios with a thin pick vs. a thick pick. Hear the difference? Try playing near the soundhole (warmer) vs. near the bridge (brighter). The position of your picking hand changes tone on acoustic.
One clean run-through of the complete solo, paying attention to tone throughout.
Tone is a physical conversation with your instrument. Learn what your guitar responds to.
✦ Your tools matter. Getting your guitar properly set up can make bends 40% easier.|
Spider crawl.

FULL PERFORMANCE — VERY SLOW (35 min): Set metronome to 40 BPM. Play the complete Hotel California solo — all 16 bars of the main solo + the full arpeggio coda + the outro — in one continuous performance. At 40 BPM, you have time to think about every note, every bend intonation, every vibrato. No rushing. No stopping. Perform it as if someone is watching. This is the foundation of musical confidence.
Record this performance. Save it. This is your Week 11 benchmark — the "everything connected" recording.
You just performed the Hotel California solo. The complete solo. From bar 1 to the final fading note. Even at 40 BPM — that is a genuine performance.
✦ This is the moment. All 84 notes connected. This is what 11 weeks of work sounds like.|
Light physical warm-up only — no heavy practice today. Give your hands a gentler session.

Put down your guitar. Close your eyes. Hear the Hotel California solo in your mind from the very first note to the final fading note. As you hear each phrase, mentally "feel" your fingers making the movements. Visualize your wrist rotating for the bends. Visualize your fingers arching for the let-ring arpeggios. Research in music pedagogy shows this mental practice is almost as effective as physical practice for memorization and consolidation.
Listen to the original Hotel California recording, following along with the tab but NOT playing. Just read + listen. Let the music map itself to the notation.
• 🎵 Follow with: [Songsterr — Hotel California Solo Tab (interactive with audio)](https://www.songsterr.com/a/wsa/eagles-hotel-california-solo-tab-s438376)
You've done the physical work. Now let your subconscious integrate it. Rest is practice.
✦ Mental rehearsal is how professionals prepare for performance. Your mind is your greatest instrument.|
Complete solo at 40 BPM as warm-up.

Play the full solo at 50 BPM. If all sections feel stable, move to 52 BPM. Then 55 BPM. Then 58 BPM. Move to the next BPM ONLY when the previous tempo is completely clean. If any section falls apart, stay at that tempo until it doesn't. Speed is not a goal — clean execution at tempo is. The BPM number is just a measurement.
At 60 BPM, do the descending triplet runs feel rushed or clean? These are your limiting factor. If they need more time, give them more time.
Speed is controlled relaxation at high tempo. Nothing more. The notes already know where to go — stop getting in their way.
✦ Don't chase the tempo. Let the tempo find you.|
Spider crawl at maximum speed you've achieved (likely 80+ BPM by now).

Yesterday's stopping point was your start today. Push from 60 to 65 BPM. Then 68 BPM. Then 70 BPM. Key watch point: Walsh's triplet runs and the 1.5-step bend. These will be your speed ceiling. If the bend sounds out of tune at higher tempos, slow down specifically for that note and practice the bend at the higher tempo in isolation.
At 70 BPM, does Walsh's section sound mechanical? Inject some dynamics — play some notes louder, some softer. Speed without expression is just exercise.
At 70 BPM, you're approaching the sound of the original recording. You're inside the song now.
✦ At 70 BPM, the Hotel California solo starts to feel like itself. You're almost there.|
Complete solo at 65 BPM as warm-up.

75 BPM is the approximate tempo of the original Hotel California recording. Play the arpeggio section specifically at 75 BPM — this is the most manageable section at full tempo. Then work on the Felder section (bars 1-8) at 75 BPM. The bends and vibrato should still be in tune and expressive even at this tempo. If anything feels "scrambled," drop back 5 BPM.
If 75 BPM isn't clean yet for every section — that is completely fine. The arpeggios may hit 75 BPM before the Walsh triplets. Different sections reach tempo at different times. This is normal.
• 🎵 Play along with: [Songsterr — Hotel California Solo (interactive tab at song tempo)](https://www.songsterr.com/a/wsa/eagles-hotel-california-solo-tab-s438376)
75 BPM. That is the Hotel California solo. You are playing the Hotel California solo.
✦ 75 BPM. The original tempo. If you're here — even on the Felder section only — you have achieved something extraordinary.|
Arpeggio cycle × 3 at current best tempo.

Go through the complete solo and mark every phrase as SOFT, MEDIUM, or AGGRESSIVE. Felder's opening bends = Soft → Medium. The ghost bends = Soft. Walsh's entrance bend = Aggressive. The double-stops = Medium. The triplet runs = Aggressive. The arpeggios = builds from Medium to Aggressive. Then play the full solo following this dynamics map.

Re-play the first 8 bars of the Felder section. This time, think about the feeling behind each phrase. "Searching" for the first bend. "Restless" on the F#7. "Hopeful" on the E major. Let the emotion inform your pick pressure and vibrato width.
Dynamics are emotional honesty made audible. The guitar says what words cannot.
✦ Dynamics are the emotional truth of music. Without them, notes are just sounds.|
Spider crawl + gentle stretch.

Play the complete Hotel California solo — from the first note to the final fading note — three times in a row, with a 2-minute rest between each performance. Treat each run as a real performance. Stand if you normally sit. Take a breath before you start. Play it as a story, not an exercise.
After all three performances, note: which run felt the most natural? Which section consistently felt weakest? That section is your final practice target for Day 83.
Performing three times in a row simulates a live show. Your hands, your memory, your confidence all strengthen under this pressure.
✦ Three performances in a row. This is what professional preparation looks like.|
Your one section that still feels on the edge of your ability — identified yesterday. Drill it one final time.

Record your best performance of the complete Hotel California solo. Do it multiple times if needed — keep the best take. This is your 12-week final recording. Compare it to your Day 7 recording, your Day 28 recording, your Day 56 recording. Hear the journey in your own playing.

Where are you? You may be at 65-70% of the full speed. You may have the arpeggios sounding beautiful but the Walsh runs still catching up. Whatever you have — it is real, it is yours, and it represents 83 days of consistent dedication.

Learning is not linear and it never ends at 12 weeks. The neural pathways you've built continue to consolidate for months after you stop practicing intensively. Keep playing. The solo will keep improving on its own.
✦ There is no "finished." There is only better than yesterday. And you are vastly better than Day 1.|

title: Mastery Performance — Play Along with the Original  \

tasks.content: The entire solo, once, at your best tempo. This is the final warm-up of the 12 weeks.

Play along with the original Hotel California recording. Not to keep up — to feel the groove of the real thing. Let the song carry you. When Felder plays his opening bend, play yours. When Walsh enters, match his energy. When the arpeggios cascade, let yours cascade too. You don't have to be at the same tempo — play at YOUR tempo while feeling the emotional arc of the song.
• 🎵 Use the interactive tab while playing along: [Songsterr — Hotel California Solo Tab](https://www.songsterr.com/a/wsa/eagles-hotel-california-solo-tab-s438376)

Put your guitar down. Think about Day 1 — when you had never played a spider crawl, had no idea what B harmonic minor was, had never done a ghost bend. Now you have all of that. You built it one day at a time. This is how mastery works: it's invisible while you're in it, and astonishing when you look back.

The Hotel California solo is now yours to keep improving indefinitely. Play it every day for 10 minutes and it will grow. Learn the notes you find hardest in the Walsh section. Try playing the full solo slightly faster each week. There's no ceiling. Just keep going.
✦ Day 84. You played the Hotel California solo on acoustic guitar. That is real. That is yours. Welcome to the other side.|
